sentences.text: And just because she was not Jewish, he did not marry her.
And he met this girl in California when he was 40 years old.
He was a great intellectual.
Nobody is good enough for him.
And somehow, he said, gosh, it's like two heads and one mind.
And both decided to go to Israel and live in Israel.
I am Jenny Singer.
Zenia Lidychower.
70 Years.
I was born in Rowne, Poland.
Yes.
Rowne is a very nice cultural city.
It had 85,000 population, half of it, maybe even more, was Jewish population.
Actually, we lived in [NON-ENGLISH], where my father had a mill.
But before my mother gave birth to me, my grandparents, the parents of my mother, insisted she should come to Rowne.
They told they'll take care of my sister and my mother.
And after a couple of weeks that I was born, we moved back to [NON-ENGLISH].
And my father, unfortunately, died when I was six or seven months old.
And my mother and my sister and myself, we moved back to Rowne.
And we stayed with our grandparents.
We were very comfortable.
My grandfather was a merchant with wheat export/import.
And I also had a rich uncle.
My mother's brother, oldest brother, was also a merchant.
And he didn't have any children.
Matter of fact, he wanted to adopt me, but my mother didn't want him.
But he was very nice to us.
And we didn't lack anything.
We had a nice cultural life.
When I was seven years old, my mother went to Czechoslovakia, to Carlsbad, and she met this very nice gentlemen, Ziggi Sardovski, who was an engineer.
And he did not know anything about Judaism.
It was before the holidays of Pesach.
And my mother invited him to come and to meet the family, which he did.
And he was really very, very excited with the traditions because he was never exposed to it.
And he fell in love with Judaism.
But the most important thing, he fell in love with my mother.
And after a brief time, they were married.
He was very good to myself and to my sister.
We called him papa.
And as a young child, I remember some people said, he's a young man.
How come that my mother doesn't have children with him?
But my mother and him were very intelligent people.
My mother explained to him that he loves us as we were his children, as long he doesn't have any other children.
And somehow, he consented.
And we moved out from our grandfather's house.
We bought a house in [NON-ENGLISH], in a very nice neighborhood.
My sister and I were going to school.
I was going to a government school in the beginning, in [NON-ENGLISH].
But I encountered some anti-Semitism because I was a very good student and the teachers always praised me.
And I remember, it was the end of a school year, and the teacher wrote into my album at the very end of the year-- he said-- he said it in Polish, but I'll translate.
He said I should study harder because knowledge is the key to the world.
And if I'll study, I should be able to open up the door to world and its great mysteries.
And at the very end, he wrote, to a brilliant and constantly smiling pupil, [NON-ENGLISH] Musokevicz.
Musokevicz was his name.
But, so sorry, the smile didn't last forever.
And in 1939, the foreign minister from Russia, Molotov, and the foreign minister from Germany, Ribbentrop, signed a pact of non-aggression.
And they divided Poland into two parts.
The Eastern part of Poland, where I was born, where I lived, became Russia.
And the Western part the Germans took over.
Well, then an evil story started.
Somehow, the Russians considered us bourgeois, even we were not.
My uncle was rich, but we were just middle class people.
And right away, the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that's like the CIA here-- took over our house and we had to move in to the grandparents.
And they took over the grandparents' house, and we wandered like gypsies.
But my sister and I did attend school.
And then, we received the passport number 11, which meant that we are not good element for communist Russia.
And we had to move to Ostrog.
Ostrog was closer to the Russian border.
And, in a few months, we had to be sent to Siberia.
My mother and my father had to go to court.
And my mother spoke fluently Russian.
She graduated gymnasium in Odessa.
And she was an intelligent woman.
She made a very good impression on the judge.
And the judge said, no, a woman with your talent, with your quality, with your knowledge of the language and your outlook at life should not be wasted in Siberia.
And they sent us back to our city, where we rented an apartment and we lived until 1941. 1941, September, the Germans came.
And really, fear and panic struck the whole Jewish community.
As soon as the Germans came, the city, especially the Jewish quarter, were bombarded day and night.
And myself, and the whole family, we ran to a cemetery where we spent two days.
Then we came back to the city.
And right away, there were orders that all Jews should give all the gold, silver, chrome, copper, bicycles, and radios to the Germans.
My father worked with this Russian engineer whose name was Gavrilov, and they were very friendly.
And even my mother and Mrs.
Gavrilov were friendly.
They also had two children approximately my age or my sister's.
And Mr.
Gavrilov told my father, why should you supply your blood enemy with all the goodies?
Give them just a token of it.
And the rest, you can come to me, and we'll build a bunker, and we'll put away everything.
And in case an emergency will come, you'll come to us.
And sure enough, my father and Mr.
Gavrilov, they lived in the outskirts of Rowne.
They had a nice house.
They took out from the veranda a few boards, and supported it with pillars, and put in blankets, and benches, and put some dry food-- dry bread.
We should be able to live in case of an emergency.
And we remembered it.
We thought if the time will come, we'll use it.
In the meantime, there were also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] notes all over the city that all the-- all the intelligentsia and all professional people should meet.
I don't remember, it was a temple or was it a school?
Because they want to get ideas how to make a ghetto or where to make the ghetto.
And sure enough, my father went also, and he never came back.
The Germans figured that as soon they'll get rid of the intellectuals of the intelligentsia, the masses-- they'll be able to do with the masses whatever that would be convenient for them.
And they told the people they need them for the German army.
And they'll get paid well, and they'll be able to send money for their families, which never happened.
A few days after this, peasants came, and they told us that all those people were shot.
This was 1941.
And my-- we all moved in a ghetto.
We were 12 people and two rooms and a kitchen.
It was very, very congested.
With small babies, we are the oldest two children, my sister and myself.
Everything was rations.
We had to get so much and so much bread, so much and so much this.
And we were-- all the time, we heard something, that another Jew was caught.
And they set his beard on fire.
And some Germans, just for tricks, for kicks, they shot through the window and killed people.
And it was really very, very scary to live.
And one day, I remember-- yes, many times, the Germans used to come at night and knock on the windows and take nice girls out.
Sometimes, they raped them.
They killed them right away.
And, sometimes, they said, we'll visit you again.
And one night, we heard a knock on our apartment.
And we knew already, but my sister and I, we never disrobed.
We always were thinking, in case we'll have to run, we should be ready to run.
And my mother tried-- did not answer on the start.
They said, if you're not going to open the door, we'll break in.
And in the meantime, it was a long, long corridor in this apartment.
And we ran-- my sister and I-- we ran through the back door.
And not far from us was a hospital, a Jewish hospital, [NON-ENGLISH] Hospital.
And my sister and I put on a white coat and cap and put on glasses.
And they sent me to the kitchen to wash the floors in the kitchen.
Well, when we came back the following day, my mother was in agony.
They beat her up and they broke her arm.
My sister was working for Germans.
She was cleaning, washing offices, washing laundry.
My mother was in the administration at the hospital.
And I read poetry in the hospital to sick people, and, also, stories to children.
And I couldn't stand-- Pardon me.
I couldn't stand the agony that my mother was.
And we were told that, in the black market, in the Christian part of the city, you can get some morphine-- painkillers.
And my mother still had a chain, a necklace, a pearl necklace.
And I took that pearl necklace and I wanted to smuggle out of the ghetto to try to trade it in for morphine.
In the morning, when everybody went to work, I also went on the bus.
And when I got out the bus, I had long braids.
I let my braids loose because all the Jews had to wear patches by us, a yellow patch in the back, and a yellow patch in the front.
And I tried to conceal the patches by unbraiding, letting my hair loose.
And I came to the market, and there was this Polish guy.
And he said, what I want?
And I told him.
And he said, show me the necklace.
And I showed him the necklace.
And he said, well, you'll come tomorrow the same place and you'll get your morphine.
And I knew already that he just cheated it out from me.
And I started to talk to him.
Why?
I want it now.
You told me you'll give it to me.
He said, you have no choice.
Because if you'll talk too long, you'll be arrested.
And somehow, I was under the impression that somebody is watching us.
And sure enough, if it was prearranged or not-- I think it was prearranged.
This Ukrainian policeman came to me.
And he took me to the police.
And he told something to the German authorities.
I don't know what he told them.
But the German took his rifle, he started to hit me in the face.
He knocked several teeth out, and my nose started to bleed.
Blood was gushing from my nose and from my mouth.
And I told him to kill me, but he said no.
You'll struggle a little longer.
And until today, I don't remember how I got back into the ghetto and to the hospital.
And the only thing they could do in the hospital is to put ice packs on my nose.
And my nose is crooked until today.
And I had a few broken teeth loose and they pulled them out, extracted the teeth.
And for months, I looked like a misfit.
You know, a 16-year-old girl without front teeth, with a crooked, but nothing could be done.
And it was November already, and there were rumors that they're going to-- the people that do not have labor cards are going to be sent to a labor camp.
I didn't have any labor papers.
So my sister and my mother said-- my sister had, and my mother-- that they'll go with me.
But unfortunately, maybe fortunately, one person died in the hospital, and they gave me her labor card.
And it was-- that's the way I remained.
But 17,000 Jews, they were evacuated into this [NON-ENGLISH].
That's what the woods' called.
They made them dig graves.
And later, they put them in the graves with grenades.
And they were all killed.
This was November, the end of November 1941.
And even nature didn't help because it was raining and snowing and hailing.
And there were dirt roads.
And the people were walking.
And they told the people, even if you didn't declare all your gold and silver or precious things, you can take it with you.
And whoever-- people carried-- they carried-- they carried food, carried luggage, carried clothing.
And it was a struggle for them.
Some of them escaped, very few, because the Germans had dogs.
And if you escaped, and the dog ran right after you, and they killed you anyway.
And after this, we came-- whoever survived survived.
In the ghetto, they didn't touch the people that had the labor cards.
And I started to work already also, outside cleaning German offices and floors, washing for the Germans, washing their laundry.
And we were already less people in our ghetto.
But people from-- and Rowne, it was the first-- they called it actually the first time that they killed-- there was a mass-- mass-- mass killing of so many people at once.
But later on, it happened in all other little villages and cities around Rowne.
And the people knew already.
So many people escaped and they came to our city.
And in our city, there was this good German who worked for the mayor from the city, [NON-ENGLISH].
And he helped some Jews he know to get-- more Jews came to the city, and we didn't have ration cards for them, you know.
So it was, really, many of us were starving because we shared with others.
So I had an uncle there, a very rich uncle.
And when the Germans came, he was petrified.
He was a very brave man.
And he was always running from one part of the ghetto to the other one.
He was afraid he shouldn't be caught.
And finally, he was caught.
And he was caught by that good German.
And my uncle spoke fluently German.
And this German appointed him.
He made him a liaison between the [NON-ENGLISH], which means mayor's office and the Judenrat, the Jewish committee.
And my uncle saved many Jews.
He brought them over from other little towns and they stayed in Rowne.
But once my uncle overheard a conversation that they are going to liquidate the ghetto.
And he came and he said whoever works out of the ghetto should not return to the ghetto.
So many Jews tried not to come back.
And it happened, it was just a-- it was a story.
The second time, a few weeks goes by, and he heard the same rumors.
And he told people.
The people didn't believe.
They said that he went berserk, and he's crazy, and he is making up stories.
And the third time, when it really happened, nobody wanted to believe him.
But then he came, but my mother believed him, and we believed him.
Even my uncles and aunts, they didn't want to believe him.
And we decided that we would go to the Gavrilov, who made a bunker for us and promised that he'll be able, in case of emergency, to stay there.
And then we came.
Mrs-- my mother knocked on the door.
And Mrs.
Gavrilov came out.
And she said, with a very harsh voice, what do you want.
And my mother said, do you remember our conversation so many months ago.
And do you remember what your husband promised us?
We have the bunker.
We would like to sleep over a night or two.
And she said just get lost.
And my mother, with tears in her eyes, begged her.
At least-- at least take the two girls, myself and my sister.
And she said, if you'll keep being persistence and you'll keep nagging me, she said, I am entertaining Gestapo men, and I'll send them out.
And we had to leave.
So my sister suggested we should go to the building where she works and we'll sleep over at the attic.
And my mother declined to come with us because she thought maybe two young girls, maybe we'll help ourselves.
But we begged her, and she said that she's going to the [NON-ENGLISH], who used to give us piano lessons.
And she said, if you want-- and she was sure that he'll take her in.
And she said, when everything will pass, we should know where to look for her.
Sure enough, we came in the attic.
We stayed one night.
And the second day, we were hungry and thirsty.
And my sister went through till the back door, to the garbage place, and she picked up some hard bread and brought.
And at the attic, there was an opening.
It was not a window, but just an opening.
A few bricks were taken out.
And I was a curious individual and I walked over.
And I looked across the street.
It was July 13, 1942.
And I saw Germans laying in bathing suits.
And I was petrified.
I thought that they noticed me.
And I was afraid to tell my sister.
And I walked over to her and I said, gosh, if we would have to run from here, where would we go.
And she said, we will go to tonight to Nadia Honovska.
She used to be a maid in our house.
And I remember they said she lives in Piekna.
Still, the name of the street is Piekna.
And I did not remember the number.
And my sister said Piekna 16.
And as she said that-- she said those words, we heard footsteps.
And Germans came up in the attic and they started to [GERMAN], you know?
And I yell-- I ran first.
And I thought my sister ran after me.
Well, my sister, they kept.
Let me run because I looked like a misfit, you know, with a broken nose and without teeth.
And I went to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the-- who I thought my mother was there.
And he said, no, your mother's not here.
Then I went to a Czech woman, [NON-ENGLISH].
And when I came in, the woman got-- she looked like a ghost and said, why did you come here.
But my uncle was hiding, and my aunt were hiding in her apartment-- in this room.
And my uncle called out from underneath the bed.
And my aunt came out from a closet.
And they begged the lady to take me.
And she said-- I'll go out, my uncle said.
And keep my niece here.
And she said, no, I am afraid.
I don't know, maybe somebody from the neighbor's noticed.
And I had to leave.
And I left.
And I left.
And I went.
And I thought, I am going to go to Nadia Honovska.
And when I came to that Nadia Honovska, no, didn't, sorry.
I didn't know what to do.
And I was going from place to place, and then I was caught.
I was caught by Germans.
And they still took me to out of town, you know, also to the woods, to [NON-ENGLISH], where the first 17,000 people were liquidated.
They took me until everybody was already in graves.
They were talking themselves, what should they do with me?
They threw me also in the grave.
And they started to cover that grave with earth.
And it started to rain, and thunder and lightnings.
And they didn't cover completely that grave.
And they threw a few grenades and they left.
And I was not killed.
I heard the screams and the cries from people, from many, many people.
But somehow, I was not-- I was not dead.
I got up.
And I pushed through, because there was very little earth in the grave.
And I pushed through the grave.
And I was standing in the rain.
And I let the rain wash-- wash over the blood from my face and my head, from my body, from my clothing.
And I saw two other people, two other girls.
And we said, let's go.
But one girl was hysterical.
She said, no.
I am going to go.
My mother's here, my father's here, and my sisters are here, and my little brother is here.
She said, I am going to stay with them.
I am not going to go.
The other girl said, yes, let's go.
And that time, I decided-- she said she's going to some Christians.
And I decided to go to Nadia Honovska.
And it took me a few days to come to her because I had to walk only when it was twilight.
I was afraid I shouldn't be caught again and recognized.
And when I came to Nadia, she said, yes.
She said, your parents and your grandparents were good to my parents and to me.
And I am going to hide you.
You're going to stay with me.
You'll be in the attic.
And many times, you know, there was no toilet, and I had to go.
And she had to take out the pails of it.
And she came, she started to curse.
And she got very nervous.
And she used to pull me from the attic into the house and hit me with an iron stick that she shovelled coal in the oven.
And she hit me.
She pulled my hair.
And she tore my dress.
And she said in Polish, [POLISH], meaning that you Jewish ghost, just get lost.
And she would chase me out.
But she was afraid that if the Germans will find and they'll torture me so much, so long, that I'll have to reveal where I was until now.
And I was by her for a year sitting in the attic.
And, finally, she really wanted to get rid of me.
So I sent her to the Czech woman where my uncle was hiding.
And my uncle started to send money.
Every second week, he sent money in the letter.
And I used to give that Nadia all the money.
And one day, my aunt asked me in the letter if I saved up some money for an emergency.
And no, I was young and didn't think about it.
To sit all day long in the roof, summertime and wintertime, I was very cold.
In summertime, hot.
And she said-- and I used to-- so that man came, and he went out in the attic.
He gave me money, one envelope for her and one little bundle for me.
And I put it in the beams, between the beams in the attic.
And then, Nadia decided I should be a little useful.
I should cook for the children.
She said a six-year-old boy and a three-year-old girl.
And she told them that I am a teacher.
And I taught the children how to read and write, the little boy.
But whenever I had to cook, I had to go to the higher part of the attic.
Because I sat-- the attic is like a triangle apartment.
So I sat in the very low part.
And when I came to the higher part of the attic, there was boards.
And across the boards, there was another woman was living, a family, [NON-ENGLISH].
And whenever I used to take call, I used to see her.
So I thought to myself, if I see her, she must see me, too.
But I didn't want to tell Nadia this because I figured at least she has some use to me that I prepare-- she worked with the Germans.
And her husband was someplace on the road working also.
So I said, as long as she'll have use for me, maybe she wouldn't chase me out.
And still, she felt very, very uncomfortable and very nervous.
Because if Christian people were hiding Jewish people and they find them-- by Christian people, Jewish people, they hang the Jews and the Christians together.
And she said that my parents were good to her, but what would happen to her children if she would be killed.
And I could understand her, too.
And one day, she used to hit me so much.
I said, so kill me already.
But she said, no, I cannot do it.
I am a Christian, she said.
I cannot kill you.
And one day, it was Sunday.
She was home.
And I was sitting in the attic.
And she came out and she said that her boy, Boloch, the six-year-old boy, he didn't know that I am a Jewish girl.
That he said that the Germans are looking for a Jewish girl.
And she said I should run.
And I didn't believe her and I didn't want to.
And she started to cross herself.
And she said if everything will happen, you'll come back.
And I was ready to leave.
And then I recall that I have some money.
So I took the money and I went down.
When I ran down the staircase, there was an exit to the right and one to the left.
And I wanted to go to the right, but, somehow, my instinct told me to go to the left.
And as soon as I left, I saw two Germans.
The-- SS man coming up.
And if they would have just took one look at me, they would know who I am because I looked like a crazy woman, you know, with messy hair, long and short.
Because she always pulled me, for a year and two months, you know, pulled me by my hair.
And my dress was all torn.
I'm white as a ghost.
And I ran.
And I went to her sister, [NON-ENGLISH].
But the two sisters weren't on speaking terms.
And as soon as I came to-- [NON-ENGLISH] said, where were you until now.
And I said, I was hiding here and here.
And she said, you were not in Nadia's house.
And I said no.
So she said, OK, come up in the attic.
And she brought me some food and I ate.
And two days passed by, and Nadia came looking for me.
Because she is afraid that I maybe fallen into the hands of Gestapo.
And then she told her-- her sister told her that I am not there.
But as soon as Nadia left, I had to leave the place, too.
And I just didn't know where to go.
And there were caves from clay, you know?
So with rocks, I dig, and dig, and dig.
And I made a cave deeper and deeper, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until I felt safe in the cave.
